_purpose: Educational deep-dive cards for the Knowledge Hub page 4 (Knowledge Cards). Two cards per theme, fourteen total. Sourced from Ellen MacArthur Foundation, UNEP, Quantis 2018, Textile Exchange, Levi Strauss LCA, Australian Bureau of Statistics, and ACCC greenwashing reports.

cards.title: Fashion is responsible for up to 8% of global carbon emissions
cards.summary: Bigger than aviation and shipping combined — and it's still growing.
body: The global fashion industry produces between 2% and 8% of all human-caused greenhouse-gas emissions. The upper end of that range puts fashion ahead of international aviation and maritime shipping combined.

It also drinks deep: an estimated 215 trillion litres of water are used by the industry each year, and about 15,000 different chemicals are applied across the supply chain — from pesticides on cotton fields to dyes and finishes in fabric mills.
On current trajectory the sector's emissions are projected to rise by more than 50% by 2030 unless production volumes and material choices change.
cards.source: UNEP — The Environmental Cost of Fashion (2019); Quantis 2018

cards.title: Most of your wardrobe is made from oil
cards.summary: Polyester, nylon and acrylic are plastics — and they now dominate clothing.
body: Synthetic fibres make up around 60% of all clothing produced today. Polyester alone accounts for more than half of global fibre production, and like nylon and acrylic, it is derived from crude oil.

Producing one kilogram of virgin polyester releases roughly 27 kg of CO₂ — about three times the footprint of cotton on a per-kilogram basis. The fossil-fuel feedstock also means these fibres never fully biodegrade; they persist in landfill and oceans for centuries.
Every wash cycle of a synthetic garment also sheds microscopic plastic fibres that flow through wastewater treatment plants and into rivers, oceans and ultimately the food chain.
cards.source: Textile Exchange — Preferred Fiber & Materials Market Report 2023

cards.title: One cotton T-shirt = 2.5 years of drinking water
cards.summary: It takes ~2,700 litres of water to grow the cotton in a single shirt.
body: Cotton is one of the thirstiest crops on the planet. Producing the cotton for a single T-shirt takes about 2,700 litres of water — roughly what one person drinks over two and a half years.

Most of that water goes into irrigating cotton fields in regions that are already water-stressed. The Aral Sea in Central Asia, once the world's fourth-largest inland body of water, has shrunk by more than 90% — driven largely by cotton irrigation diverted from its feeder rivers.
Switching to organic, rainfed, or recycled cotton cuts that water footprint by 80–90%, while keeping the same comfort and breathability.
cards.source: WWF — The Impact of a Cotton T-Shirt; UNEP 2019

cards.title: Less than 4% of fibres, but 75% of the methane
cards.summary: Animal fibres are a sliver of the wardrobe — and a huge slice of the impact.
body: Wool, leather, silk, cashmere and other animal-derived materials together make up less than 4% of global fibre production. Yet they are responsible for an estimated three-quarters of fashion's biogenic methane emissions.

The reason is livestock: sheep and goats release methane through digestion, and methane is more than 80 times more potent than CO₂ over a 20-year horizon. Cashmere goats in particular have caused severe overgrazing across the Mongolian steppe, driving desertification.
Recycled wool, deadstock leather, and plant-based alternatives like Tencel-blended knitwear offer the warmth and feel of animal fibres at a fraction of the environmental cost.
cards.source: Collective Fashion Justice — CIRCUMFAUNA Initiative; FAO Livestock Methane Report

cards.title: Dyeing causes 20% of the world's water pollution
cards.summary: The colour in your jeans came from a river somewhere.
body: Textile dyeing and finishing is the second-largest polluter of clean water globally, responsible for roughly 20% of all industrial wastewater pollution. In countries with weak environmental enforcement, untreated dye effluent often runs straight into rivers — colouring them visibly red, blue or black.
A single pair of jeans can require 70+ litres of water just for dyeing, alongside heavy metals, formaldehyde, and azo dyes that are linked to cancer and skin disorders in textile workers.

Closed-loop dyeing systems and waterless techniques like CO₂ dyeing exist and use up to 95% less water — but they remain rare because they require capital investment most factories can't afford on tight fast-fashion margins.
cards.source: World Bank — Pollution from the Textile Industry; Ellen MacArthur Foundation

cards.title: Up to 30% of clothes made are never sold
cards.summary: Burned, shredded, or buried — overproduction is the industry's open secret.
body: The fashion industry consistently produces 20–30% more clothing than it sells. Excess stock is routinely incinerated, shredded, or sent to landfill — often by luxury brands protecting brand exclusivity.

Burberry famously admitted to burning £28 million of unsold stock in a single year before public outcry forced a policy change in 2018. France has since banned the destruction of unsold clothing, but most other countries have no such law.
On-demand manufacturing, smaller seasonal drops, and made-to-order capsules are emerging as alternatives — but they remain a tiny share of the global market.
cards.source: Ellen MacArthur Foundation — A New Textiles Economy

cards.title: We buy twice as much, but wear it half as long
cards.summary: Clothing production has doubled in 20 years; how often we wear each piece has dropped 40%.
body: Between 2000 and 2015, global garment production roughly doubled. Over the same period, the average number of times a piece of clothing was worn before being discarded fell by about 40%.

Fast-fashion brands now release 12 to 24 collections a year, compared with the traditional two seasons. Garments are designed to feel disposable — and the price points reinforce that, with shirts priced lower than a coffee.
The result is a wardrobe that grows faster than ever, while individual items leave it sooner than ever. The gap between what we own and what we actually wear has never been wider.
cards.source: Ellen MacArthur Foundation 2017; McKinsey State of Fashion

cards.title: Wearing a garment 9 months longer cuts its impact by 20–30%
cards.summary: Use what you already own — it's the highest-leverage move you can make.
body: WRAP's landmark Valuing Our Clothes study found that extending the active life of a garment by just nine months reduces its carbon, water and waste footprints by 20–30% each.

That's a bigger reduction than switching to organic cotton or recycled polyester would deliver. It's also the easiest: it requires no new purchase, no certification scheme, no logistics — just a willingness to repair, restyle and rewear.
Buying second-hand, mending small holes, and learning a single garment-care skill (like correct washing temperature) all extend life. The most sustainable garment is the one already in your wardrobe.

cards.title: Washing your jeans accounts for 37% of their lifetime carbon
cards.summary: Levi's tracked one pair of 501s for years — the dryer was the biggest culprit.
body: Levi Strauss & Co conducted a full life-cycle assessment on a pair of 501 jeans and found that consumer use — washing, drying and ironing — accounted for 37% of the garment's total carbon footprint, more than the cotton farming or manufacturing stages.
The biggest single contributor is tumble drying. A single dryer cycle uses around 5x more energy than the wash itself. Hot-water washing also dwarfs cold-water washing in energy use.
Switching to cold-wash, line-drying, and washing jeans every 10 wears instead of every 2 cuts the in-use footprint by more than half — without changing the garment at all.
cards.source: Levi Strauss & Co — Lifecycle of a Jean (2015)

cards.title: Small loads release 5x more microfibres
cards.summary: Half-empty washing machines tumble synthetic clothes harder — and shed more plastic.
body: Researchers at the University of Newcastle found that washing a small load of synthetic clothes releases roughly five times more microfibres than washing a full load. The reason is mechanical: in a half-empty drum, fabric tumbles further and abrades against itself more aggressively.
A single laundry load of synthetic garments can release up to 700,000 microscopic plastic fibres. They pass through wastewater treatment plants and accumulate in oceans and freshwater systems.
Washing full loads at 30°C, using a microfibre-catching wash bag (like Guppyfriend), and choosing natural fibres for items washed frequently are the simplest interventions.

cards.title: Australia recycles only 7% of its used clothing
cards.summary: We send 200,000 tonnes of textiles to landfill every year — most still wearable.
body: Australians are among the largest textile consumers per capita in the world, buying an average of 27 kg of new clothing each year. Yet only about 7% of that ends up in any form of recycling or reuse system.

More than 200,000 tonnes of clothing — much of it still wearable — is sent to Australian landfill every year. In landfill, synthetic fibres release methane as they break down, and dyes and chemicals leach into soil and groundwater.

Charity bins are not a get-out-of-guilt-free card either: Australian charities export or destroy roughly 60% of the clothing donated to them, because the supply far exceeds local demand for second-hand goods.
cards.source: Australian Bureau of Statistics; Australia's National Clothing Product Stewardship Scheme 2023

cards.title: A garbage truck of clothing is landfilled every second
cards.summary: Globally, the equivalent of one full garbage truck of textiles is dumped or burned every second.
body: Worldwide, the equivalent of one garbage truck of textiles is sent to landfill or incinerated every single second. That works out to over 92 million tonnes of textile waste per year — and the figure keeps climbing as fast-fashion volumes grow.

The Atacama Desert in Chile has become an unofficial dumping ground for the world's discarded fashion. Satellite images now show a textile pile visible from space — a mound of unsold and donated clothing that has accumulated over the last decade.
Less than 1% of material used in clothing is recycled into new clothing. The rest is downcycled into insulation or wiping cloths, exported, or simply burned.
cards.source: Ellen MacArthur Foundation 2017; AFP / Atacama Desert reporting

cards.title: "Bamboo" clothing is usually rebranded viscose
cards.summary: If a label says "bamboo" without certification, it's most likely the dirtiest fibre on the shelf.
body: Most clothing sold as "bamboo" is actually viscose rayon made from bamboo pulp — and the conversion process uses carbon disulfide and other toxic chemicals that are released into the air and water around the mills.

The US Federal Trade Commission has fined multiple major retailers for marketing viscose-from-bamboo as natural "bamboo fibre". In Australia, the ACCC has issued similar guidance: claims about bamboo must distinguish between mechanically-processed bamboo (rare and rough) and chemically-processed viscose (common and polluting).

Genuinely cleaner alternatives exist — Tencel Lyocell, made from FSC-certified wood pulp in a closed-loop solvent process, recovers 99% of its solvent and is what "sustainable bamboo" is usually pretending to be.
cards.source: US FTC — Bamboo Marketing Guidelines; ACCC Greenwashing Guidance 2023

cards.title: Vague green claims now require proof in Australia
cards.summary: The ACCC found 57% of green claims by fashion brands were misleading or unsubstantiated.
body: An ACCC sweep in 2023 reviewed environmental marketing claims across Australian businesses, including fashion. It found that 57% of claims raised concerns — using vague language like "eco-friendly", "conscious", or "sustainable" without any evidence to back them up.

Look for specific, third-party verified claims instead. Trustworthy signals include: GOTS (Global Organic Textile Standard) for organic fibres, GRS (Global Recycled Standard) for recycled content, OEKO-TEX for chemical safety, and B Corp for company-wide accountability.

If a brand can't tell you which factory made the garment, what percentage of it is recycled, or how much water it took to produce — assume the green label is marketing first and accountability second.
cards.source: ACCC Internet Sweep 2023; Changing Markets Foundation — Synthetics Anonymous
